content:
# Disagreement Log -- Numbers 34 (NUM_034)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA

---

### 34:2 -- "come into" vs. "enter into" vs. "enter"; "borders" vs. "boundaries"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "Command the sons of Israel and say to them: When you come into the land of Canaan, this [is] the land that shall fall to you as an inheritance, the land of Canaan according to its borders." |
| LOGOS | "Command the sons of Israel and say to them: When you enter into the land of Canaan, this is the land that shall fall to you as an inheritance -- the land of Canaan according to its boundaries." |
| PNEUMA | "Command the sons of Israel and say to them: When you enter the land of Canaan -- this is the land that will fall to you as an inheritance, the land of Canaan according to its boundaries --" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "Command the sons of Israel and say to them: When you come into the land of Canaan, this is the land that shall fall to you as an inheritance -- the land of Canaan according to its borders." -- (1) "come into" (EMET) is adopted over "enter into" (LOGOS) and "enter" (PNEUMA) for ki tavo'u el, preserving the Hebrew directional construction with the preposition el. (2) "borders" (EMET) is adopted over "boundaries" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for gevuloteha; both are valid, but "borders" is more concise and closer to the concrete Hebrew sense of gevul as a marked boundary line. (3) PNEUMA uses "will fall" (future indicative) rather than "shall fall" (EMET/LOGOS); "shall" is adopted as appropriate for divine decree language. (4) PNEUMA restructures the clause with em-dashes setting off an appositive, departing from the Hebrew syntax; the EMET/LOGOS structure with the dash before the restatement is adopted. (5) EMET's bracketed "[is]" is dropped following the SOPHIA convention of not using brackets for implied copulas where the English reads naturally without them.

---

### 34:3 -- "alongside Edom" vs. "along the border of Edom"; "eastward" vs. "on the east"; sentence structure
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And your southern side shall be from the wilderness of Zin alongside Edom, and your southern border shall be from the end of the Salt Sea eastward." |
| LOGOS | "And your southern side shall be from the wilderness of Zin alongside Edom, and your southern boundary shall be from the end of the Salt Sea on the east." |
| PNEUMA | "your southern side shall extend from the wilderness of Zin along the border of Edom. And your southern boundary on the east shall begin from the end of the Salt Sea." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And your southern side shall be from the wilderness of Zin alongside Edom, and your southern border shall be from the end of the Salt Sea eastward." -- (1) PNEUMA omits the initial "And" and adds "shall extend," departing from the Hebrew verbless clause; the literal construction "shall be from" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted. (2) "alongside Edom" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's "along the border of Edom," which adds "border" not explicitly in the Hebrew (al yad Edom means "by the side/hand of Edom"). (3) "eastward" (EMET) for qedmah is adopted over LOGOS's "on the east," as the Hebrew directional he-suffix conveys motion/direction ("eastward") rather than static location. (4) PNEUMA restructures the verse into two separate sentences and repositions "on the east" to modify the boundary rather than the Salt Sea, departing significantly from the Hebrew word order. (5) "border" (EMET) is adopted over "boundary" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) per the resolution in verse 2.

---

### 34:4 -- "pass on" vs. "pass over" vs. "pass through"; "goings out" vs. "going out" vs. "limit"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And your border shall turn from the south to the ascent of Akrabbim and pass on to Zin, and its goings out shall be from the south to Kadesh-barnea, and it shall go out to Hazar-addar and pass on to Azmon." |
| LOGOS | "And your boundary shall turn from the south to the ascent of Akrabbim, and pass over to Zin, and its going out shall be from the south to Kadesh-barnea, and it shall go out to Hazar-addar, and pass over to Azmon." |
| PNEUMA | "And your boundary shall turn south of the Ascent of Akrabbim and pass through to Zin, and its limit shall be south of Kadesh-barnea. And it shall go out to Hazar-addar and pass through to Azmon." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And your border shall turn from the south to the ascent of Akrabbim and pass on to Zin, and its goings out shall be from the south to Kadesh-barnea, and it shall go out to Hazar-addar and pass on to Azmon." -- (1) "pass on" (EMET) for ve'avar is adopted over "pass over" (LOGOS) and "pass through" (PNEUMA); avar in this boundary context means to continue along, and "pass on" best conveys forward progression without implying crossing over or through something. (2) "goings out" (EMET) for totz'otav is adopted over "going out" (LOGOS, singular) and "limit" (PNEUMA, interpretive); the Hebrew plural totse'ot is a technical boundary term for terminal points. (3) PNEUMA capitalizes "Ascent" as part of the proper name; lower-case "ascent" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted since the Hebrew ma'aleh is a common noun describing the topographical feature. (4) PNEUMA renders "south of Kadesh-barnea" rather than "from the south to Kadesh-barnea," reinterpreting the directional phrase. The Hebrew minnegev le-Kadesh-Barnea means "from the south [the border extends] to Kadesh-barnea," not a locative "south of." (5) PNEUMA breaks the verse into two sentences; the single flowing sentence (EMET/LOGOS) preserves the Hebrew continuous boundary description.

---

### 34:5 -- "Wadi of Egypt" vs. "Brook of Egypt"; "goings out" vs. "going out" vs. "limit"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the border shall turn from Azmon to the Wadi of Egypt, and its goings out shall be toward the Sea." |
| LOGOS | "And the boundary shall turn from Azmon to the Brook of Egypt, and its going out shall be toward the sea." |
| PNEUMA | "And the boundary shall turn from Azmon to the Brook of Egypt, and its limit shall be at the Sea." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the border shall turn from Azmon to the Wadi of Egypt, and its goings out shall be toward the Sea." -- (1) "Wadi of Egypt" (EMET) is adopted over "Brook of Egypt" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for nachlah Mitsrayim. The Hebrew nachal refers to a seasonal watercourse (wadi), and "Wadi" has become the standard English term for this geographical feature (Wadi el-Arish). "Brook" is the older English convention but is misleading since a brook implies a permanent stream. (2) "goings out" (EMET, plural) is adopted over "going out" (LOGOS, singular) and "limit" (PNEUMA) for totz'otav, per the resolution in verse 4. (3) "toward the Sea" (EMET) with capital "S" is adopted; the Sea here refers specifically to the Mediterranean (the Great Sea), warranting the proper noun treatment. LOGOS uses lowercase "sea." (4) PNEUMA uses "at" rather than "toward" for the directional sense; the Hebrew is directional (toward), not locative (at).

---

### 34:6 -- "And [as for]" vs. "And as for"; "shall be [your] border" vs. "shall be your boundary" vs. "shall be your boundary"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And [as for] the western border, the Great Sea shall be [your] border; this shall be your western border." |
| LOGOS | "And as for the western boundary, you shall have the Great Sea as a boundary; this shall be your western boundary." |
| PNEUMA | "And as for the western boundary -- the Great Sea shall be your boundary. This shall be your western boundary." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And as for the western border, the Great Sea shall be your border; this shall be your western border." -- (1) EMET's brackets around "[as for]" and "[your]" are removed; the sense is clear without them and the Hebrew gevul yam implies "your" from context. (2) LOGOS restructures to "you shall have the Great Sea as a boundary," departing from the Hebrew predicate nominative construction ("the Great Sea shall be a border"); the EMET/PNEUMA structure is closer to the Hebrew. (3) PNEUMA uses an em-dash and period, breaking into two sentences; the semicolon (EMET) better preserves the Hebrew's continuous clause structure. (4) "border" adopted over "boundary" per earlier resolution.

---

### 34:7 -- "mark out for yourselves" vs. "mark out your line to"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And this shall be your northern border: from the Great Sea you shall mark out for yourselves Mount Hor." |
| LOGOS | "And this shall be your northern boundary: from the Great Sea you shall mark out for yourselves Mount Hor." |
| PNEUMA | "And this shall be your northern boundary: from the Great Sea you shall mark out your line to Mount Hor." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And this shall be your northern border: from the Great Sea you shall mark out for yourselves Mount Hor." -- PNEUMA adds "your line to," interpreting the hitpael of ta'ah as drawing a line and adding "to" before Mount Hor. The Hebrew teta'u lakhem hor hahar is literally "you shall mark out for yourselves Mount Hor" (EMET/LOGOS), with the reflexive lakhem ("for yourselves") rather than a line being drawn. PNEUMA's addition is interpretive and not supported by the Hebrew syntax. "border" adopted over "boundary" per earlier resolution.

---

### 34:8 -- "mark out [the border]" vs. "mark out" vs. "mark out your line"; "entrance of Hamath" vs. "Lebo-hamath"; "goings out" vs. "going out" vs. "limit"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "From Mount Hor you shall mark out [the border] to the entrance of Hamath, and the goings out of the border shall be toward Zedad." |
| LOGOS | "From Mount Hor you shall mark out to the entrance of Hamath, and the going out of the boundary shall be toward Zedad." |
| PNEUMA | "From Mount Hor you shall mark out your line to Lebo-hamath, and the limit of the boundary shall be at Zedad." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "From Mount Hor you shall mark out to the entrance of Hamath, and the goings out of the border shall be toward Zedad." -- (1) EMET's bracketed "[the border]" is a clarifying addition not in the Hebrew; LOGOS's unadorned "mark out to" is cleaner and adopted. (2) "the entrance of Hamath" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's "Lebo-hamath." The Hebrew levo' Chamat consists of the infinitive construct of bo' ("to come/enter") plus the place name Hamath. While some scholars treat "Lebo-hamath" as a single place name, the traditional and majority rendering "the entrance of Hamath" preserves the Hebrew syntax. (3) "goings out" (EMET) is adopted for totz'ot per earlier resolution. (4) PNEUMA uses "at Zedad" rather than "toward Zedad"; the Hebrew directional sense (el) is "toward." (5) "limit" (PNEUMA) for totz'ot is again replaced by the literal "goings out."

---

### 34:10 -- "mark out for yourselves [as] the eastern border" vs. "mark out your eastern boundary" vs. "mark out your eastern boundary"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And you shall mark out for yourselves [as] the eastern border from Hazar-enan to Shepham." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall mark out your eastern boundary from Hazar-enan to Shepham." |
| PNEUMA | "And you shall mark out your eastern boundary from Hazar-enan to Shepham." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And you shall mark out for yourselves the eastern border from Hazar-enan to Shepham." -- EMET preserves the reflexive lakhem ("for yourselves") which LOGOS and PNEUMA omit. The Hebrew vehit'avitem lakhem explicitly includes the reflexive pronoun, so it is retained. EMET's bracketed "[as]" is dropped as unnecessary. "border" adopted over "boundary" per earlier resolution.

---

### 34:11 -- "shall strike against the shoulder" vs. "strike against the slope" vs. "descend and reach the slope"; "Kinnereth" vs. "Chinnereth"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the border shall go down from Shepham to Riblah, on the east side of Ain; and the border shall go down and shall strike against the shoulder of the Sea of Kinnereth eastward." |
| LOGOS | "And the boundary shall go down from Shepham to Riblah, on the east side of Ain. And the boundary shall go down and strike against the slope of the Sea of Chinnereth on the east." |
| PNEUMA | "And the boundary shall go down from Shepham to Riblah, on the east side of Ain. And the boundary shall descend and reach the slope on the eastern side of the Sea of Kinnereth." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the border shall go down from Shepham to Riblah, on the east side of Ain. And the border shall go down and shall strike against the shoulder of the Sea of Kinnereth eastward." -- (1) "shoulder" (EMET) is adopted over "slope" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) for ketef. The Hebrew ketef literally means "shoulder" and is used idiomatically for a geographic ridge or slope. Preserving the literal "shoulder" maintains the Hebrew's concrete body-part metaphor, consistent with the SOPHIA convention of preserving vivid Hebrew idiom. (2) "shall strike against" (EMET) is adopted over PNEUMA's "reach," which flattens the Hebrew machah ("to strike, touch upon"). LOGOS also has "strike against." (3) "Kinnereth" (EMET/PNEUMA) is adopted over "Chinnereth" (LOGOS). The Hebrew kinneret begins with the letter kaf, standardly transliterated as "K" in English. The "Ch" spelling follows older conventions but is less transparent. (4) "eastward" (EMET) with the directional suffix is adopted over "on the east" (LOGOS) and "on the eastern side" (PNEUMA) for qedmah. (5) PNEUMA's "descend" for yarad departs from the established "go down" used by EMET/LOGOS. (6) EMET joins both clauses with a semicolon; LOGOS/PNEUMA use a period. A period is adopted for clarity, as this is a long verse with two distinct boundary segments.

---

### 34:12 -- "goings out" vs. "going out" vs. "limit"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And the border shall go down to the Jordan, and its goings out shall be at the Salt Sea. This shall be your land according to its borders all around." |
| LOGOS | "And the boundary shall go down to the Jordan, and its going out shall be at the Salt Sea. This shall be your land according to its boundaries all around." |
| PNEUMA | "And the boundary shall go down to the Jordan, and its limit shall be at the Salt Sea. This shall be your land according to its boundaries all around." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And the border shall go down to the Jordan, and its goings out shall be at the Salt Sea. This shall be your land according to its borders all around." -- (1) "goings out" (EMET) for totz'otav adopted per earlier resolution. (2) "borders" over "boundaries" per earlier resolution. The three translations are otherwise in close agreement on this verse.

---

### 34:13 -- "inherit by lot" vs. "receive by lot as an inheritance"; "commanded" vs. "has commanded"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And Moses commanded the sons of Israel, saying, 'This [is] the land which you shall inherit by lot, which YHWH commanded to give to the nine tribes and the half-tribe.'" |
| LOGOS | "And Moses commanded the sons of Israel, saying, 'This is the land which you shall inherit by lot, which YHWH has commanded to give to the nine tribes and to the half-tribe.'" |
| PNEUMA | "And Moses commanded the sons of Israel, saying, 'This is the land that you shall receive by lot as an inheritance, which YHWH has commanded to give to the nine tribes and the half-tribe.'" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And Moses commanded the sons of Israel, saying, 'This is the land which you shall inherit by lot, which YHWH has commanded to give to the nine tribes and the half-tribe.'" -- (1) "inherit by lot" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's "receive by lot as an inheritance." The Hebrew titnachalu otah begoral uses the hitpael of nachal ("to take as inheritance") with begoral ("by lot"). "Inherit by lot" captures this concisely; PNEUMA's periphrastic expansion adds words not in the Hebrew. (2) "has commanded" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "commanded" (EMET) for tsivvah; the Hebrew perfect with present relevance is well served by the English present perfect. (3) LOGOS adds "to" before "the half-tribe" ("to the nine tribes and to the half-tribe"); the Hebrew has a single le- governing both, so the simpler construction without the repeated "to" (EMET/PNEUMA) is adopted. (4) PNEUMA uses "that" rather than "which" for the relative pronoun; "which" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted for consistency with formal register.

---

### 34:14 -- "house of their fathers" vs. "ancestral households"; "have received" vs. "have already received"; semicolon vs. period
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "For the tribe of the sons of Reuben according to the house of their fathers, and the tribe of the sons of Gad according to the house of their fathers, have received, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have received their inheritance." |
| LOGOS | "For the tribe of the sons of Reuben according to the house of their fathers, and the tribe of the sons of Gad according to the house of their fathers, have received their inheritance; and the half-tribe of Manasseh have received their inheritance." |
| PNEUMA | "For the tribe of the sons of Reuben according to their ancestral households, and the tribe of the sons of Gad according to their ancestral households, have already received their inheritance; and the half-tribe of Manasseh have received their inheritance." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "For the tribe of the sons of Reuben according to the house of their fathers, and the tribe of the sons of Gad according to the house of their fathers, have received their inheritance; and the half-tribe of Manasseh have received their inheritance." -- (1) "the house of their fathers" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's "their ancestral households." The Hebrew leveit avotam is literally "to/according to the house of their fathers," and the literal rendering is preferred. (2) PNEUMA adds "already" before "received," which is interpretive; while the perfect tense implies completed action, the adverb is not in the Hebrew. (3) EMET omits "their inheritance" after the first "have received," though the Hebrew lachaqu nachalatam includes it. LOGOS/PNEUMA include it, and it is adopted. (4) LOGOS uses a semicolon to separate the Manasseh clause, which is clearer than EMET's run-on; adopted.

---

### 34:15 -- "across the Jordan" vs. "beyond the Jordan"; "[at] Jericho" vs. "opposite Jericho" vs. "across from Jericho"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "The two tribes and the half-tribe have received their inheritance across the Jordan [at] Jericho eastward, toward the sunrise." |
| LOGOS | "The two tribes and the half-tribe have received their inheritance beyond the Jordan, opposite Jericho, eastward, toward the sunrise." |
| PNEUMA | "The two tribes and the half-tribe have received their inheritance beyond the Jordan, across from Jericho, eastward toward the sunrise." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "The two tribes and the half-tribe have received their inheritance beyond the Jordan, opposite Jericho, eastward, toward the sunrise." -- (1) "beyond the Jordan" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over "across the Jordan" (EMET) for me'ever laYarden. In context, the speaker is west of the Jordan describing territory to the east; "beyond" captures the sense of "on the other side of" from the speaker's perspective. (2) "opposite Jericho" (LOGOS) is adopted over EMET's "[at] Jericho" (bracketed, interpretive) and PNEUMA's "across from Jericho." The Hebrew Yericho here functions as a locative reference point, and "opposite" (LOGOS) renders the spatial relationship implied by the construction. (3) PNEUMA omits the comma between "eastward" and "toward the sunrise," joining them as a single phrase; EMET/LOGOS treat them as two parallel directional markers. The Hebrew qedmah mizrachah ha-shamesh has both directional terms, and the comma separating them (LOGOS) preserves the parallel structure.

---

### 34:17 -- "divide" vs. "apportion"; "son of Nun" vs. "the son of Nun"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "These [are] the names of the men who shall divide the land for you as an inheritance: Eleazar the priest and Joshua son of Nun." |
| LOGOS | "These are the names of the men who shall divide the land for you as an inheritance: Eleazar the priest and Joshua the son of Nun." |
| PNEUMA | "These are the names of the men who shall apportion the land for you as an inheritance: Eleazar the priest and Joshua son of Nun." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "These are the names of the men who shall divide the land for you as an inheritance: Eleazar the priest and Joshua son of Nun." -- (1) "divide" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "apportion" (PNEUMA) for yinchalu. While "apportion" captures the inheritance nuance, "divide" is the more direct rendering and has majority support. (2) "son of Nun" (EMET/PNEUMA) without the article is adopted over "the son of Nun" (LOGOS). The Hebrew bin-Nun is a construct chain without the article; "son of Nun" preserves this. (3) EMET's brackets "[are]" dropped per convention.

---

### 34:18 -- "one leader, one leader from [each] tribe you shall take" vs. "you shall take one leader from each tribe" vs. "you shall take one leader from each tribe"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And one leader, one leader from [each] tribe you shall take to divide the land as an inheritance." |
| LOGOS | "And you shall take one leader from each tribe to divide the land for an inheritance." |
| PNEUMA | "And you shall take one leader from each tribe to apportion the land as an inheritance." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And one leader, one leader from each tribe you shall take to divide the land as an inheritance." -- The Hebrew nasi echad nasi echad lematteh uses a distributive repetition ("one leader, one leader") which EMET alone preserves. LOGOS and PNEUMA smooth this to a single "one leader from each tribe," losing the emphatic Hebrew construction. The repetition is preserved per the SOPHIA convention of maintaining Hebrew rhetorical patterns. EMET's bracketed "[each]" is removed since "from each tribe" reads naturally. "divide" adopted over "apportion" (PNEUMA) per verse 17. "as an inheritance" (EMET/PNEUMA) adopted over "for an inheritance" (LOGOS); the Hebrew lenachalah uses the le- prefix which can mean either, but "as" is adopted for consistency.

---

### 34:19 -- "for the tribe of Judah" vs. "from the tribe of Judah"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And these [are] the names of the men: for the tribe of Judah, Caleb son of Jephunneh." |
| LOGOS | "And these are the names of the men: for the tribe of Judah, Caleb the son of Jephunneh." |
| PNEUMA | "And these are the names of the men: from the tribe of Judah, Caleb son of Jephunneh." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And these are the names of the men: for the tribe of Judah, Caleb son of Jephunneh." -- (1) "for the tribe" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "from the tribe" (PNEUMA) for lematteh. The Hebrew preposition le- here introduces the tribe for which the leader serves, not the origin; "for" preserves this sense. (2) "son of" without the article (EMET/PNEUMA) is adopted over "the son of" (LOGOS) per the resolution in verse 17. (3) EMET's brackets "[are]" dropped per convention.

---

### 34:22 -- "for the tribe of the sons of Dan, a leader, Bukki" vs. "for the tribe of the sons of Dan, a leader: Bukki" vs. "a leader from the tribe of the sons of Dan, Bukki"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "And for the tribe of the sons of Dan, a leader, Bukki son of Jogli." |
| LOGOS | "And for the tribe of the sons of Dan, a leader: Bukki the son of Jogli." |
| PNEUMA | "And a leader from the tribe of the sons of Dan, Bukki son of Jogli." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "And for the tribe of the sons of Dan, a leader: Bukki son of Jogli." -- (1) "for the tribe" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over PNEUMA's restructured "a leader from the tribe," which changes the Hebrew clause order. The Hebrew ulematteh venei-Dan nasi precedes the name. (2) LOGOS's colon after "a leader" is adopted over EMET's comma as it better separates the title from the name. (3) "son of" without article (EMET/PNEUMA) adopted per convention.

---

### 34:29 -- "These [are those] whom" vs. "These are the ones whom"; "divide" vs. "distribute"
| Translator | Reading |
|---|---|
| EMET | "These [are those] whom YHWH commanded to divide the inheritance for the sons of Israel in the land of Canaan." |
| LOGOS | "These are the ones whom YHWH commanded to divide the inheritance for the sons of Israel in the land of Canaan." |
| PNEUMA | "These are the ones whom YHWH commanded to distribute the inheritance to the sons of Israel in the land of Canaan." |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** "These are the ones whom YHWH commanded to divide the inheritance for the sons of Israel in the land of Canaan." -- (1) "These are the ones whom" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is adopted over EMET's "These [are those] whom" with brackets; "the ones" is a natural English equivalent of the Hebrew asher without needing bracketed supplements. (2) "divide" (EMET/LOGOS) is adopted over "distribute" (PNEUMA) for lenachel; consistency with the verb choice in verses 17-18 is maintained. (3) PNEUMA uses "to the sons of Israel" rather than "for the sons of Israel" (EMET/LOGOS) for livnei Yisra'el. The Hebrew le- here indicates beneficiary ("for"), and "for" is adopted. (4) PNEUMA presents verse 29 without quotation marks, as though it is narrator speech rather than continued divine speech. EMET/LOGOS include it within the quotation begun in verse 17. The Hebrew context supports its inclusion in YHWH's speech, so closing quotation marks are placed at the end of verse 29.
